I reviewed the SPI code and found `Spi_TestMiso()` leaves MISO configured as a GPIO output set high, so the SPI peripheral never regains control of MISO. That explains the ESP32 reading all 0xFF and the Daisy “halt” after the MISO test. I’ll update `Spi_TestMiso()` to restore the MISO pin to SPI AF (PB4, AF5 for SPI1), then re-prime/restart the DMA so the link can operate.

Tool use: **apply_patch**

<details>
        <summary>Apply patch for /workspaces/WaveX/firmware/daisy/src/comm/inter_spi.cpp</summary>
      

```diff
<<'PATCH'
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: /workspaces/WaveX/firmware/daisy/src/comm/inter_spi.cpp
@@
 void Spi_TestMiso() {
     if (s_hw) {
@@
         // Re-check CS level
         cs_level = cs_test.Read();
         s_hw->PrintLine("CS level after test = %d", cs_level ? 1 : 0);
+
+        // IMPORTANT: Restore MISO pin to SPI alternate function so the peripheral
+        // regains control. Leaving it as GPIO OUTPUT HIGH causes the master to
+        // read 0xFF bytes forever.
+        miso_test.DeInit();
+
+        // Daisy Seed SPI1 MISO is PB4 (AF5)
+        __HAL_RCC_GPIOB_CLK_ENABLE();
+        GPIO_InitTypeDef GPIO_InitStruct = {0};
+        GPIO_InitStruct.Pin = GPIO_PIN_4;
+        GPIO_InitStruct.Mode = GPIO_MODE_AF_PP;
+        GPIO_InitStruct.Pull = GPIO_NOPULL;
+        GPIO_InitStruct.Speed = GPIO_SPEED_FREQ_VERY_HIGH;
+        GPIO_InitStruct.Alternate = GPIO_AF5_SPI1;
+        HAL_GPIO_Init(GPIOB, &GPIO_InitStruct);
+
+        s_hw->PrintLine("Restored MISO (PB4) to AF5 for SPI1");
+
+        // Ensure our continuous DMA is armed again
+        g_dma_setup = false;
+        prime_tx_and_start_dma();
     }
 }
*** End Patch
PATCH
```

---

Model: GPT-5 Code Assistant, 32k, Multimodal (Code), rev 2025-08-15

I inspected the Daisy SPI code and fixed the MISO test: after your MISO GPIO toggle, MISO was left as a push-pull output held high, so the SPI peripheral never reclaimed the line. I updated `Spi_TestMiso()` to restore MISO (PB4/D9) to SPI AF5 and re-arm SPI DMA.

### What’s happening
- ESP32 logs show MISO=1 and returning all 0xFF. That’s exactly what happens if the slave drives MISO high as a GPIO or if MISO is pulled up and the peripheral isn’t in control.
- On Daisy, `Spi_TestMiso()` drove MISO as GPIO and left it HIGH (“MISO test complete - left high…”), which prevented SPI1 from using the pin for subsequent transfers, stalling the link.

### Fix applied on Daisy
- After the MISO GPIO connectivity test, MISO is now:
  - De-initialized from GPIO.
  - Reconfigured to AF5 on PB4 (SPI1 MISO).
  - Continuous DMA is re-primed to ensure slave is ready before the next CS falling edge.

### What to try now
- Rebuild/flash Daisy and repeat your test sequence:
  1) Run the MISO/CS connectivity test.
  2) Immediately after, the log should include “Restored MISO (PB4) to AF5 for SPI1”.
  3) ESP32 small 8-byte read should no longer be all `FF`. You should see a sane header (not `type=0xFFFF len=65535 crc=0xFFFF`).
- If you still get 0xFF:
  - Power-cycle Daisy to guarantee pins are reset.
  - Ensure no other code reconfigures `D9` (PB4) back to GPIO.
  - Confirm ESP32 is using Mode 0 and that CS is wired to the Daisy’s hardware NSS (PA4 for SPI1 in libDaisy config).

### Notes
- You have two SPI paths in the repo (`inter_spi.cpp` using libDaisy SPI1 and `inter_spi_hal.cpp` using raw HAL on SPI3). Make sure only one is active at a time in your build; mixing them can fight over pins.
- ESP32’s “periodic SPI poke” will continue to read 0xFF if Daisy hasn’t reclaimed MISO; after this fix it should read actual frame bytes.

- I updated `Spi_TestMiso()` in `firmware/daisy/src/comm/inter_spi.cpp` to reconfigure `PB4` back to AF5 (SPI1 MISO) and re-prime DMA.

Model: GPT-5 Code Assistant, 32k, Multimodal (Code), rev 2025-08-15

I looked at `main.cpp`, SPI pin config, and your latest logs. Daisy still returns 0xFF and reports IRQ=ASSERTED while ESP32 reads DaisyIRQ=1, which points to two issues: MISO not actually being driven by SPI, and an IRQ wiring/polarity mismatch.

What to do next (quick, high-signal):
- Confirm SPI pins/wiring
  - Daisy: `D8(SCK) <- ESP SCLK16`, `D10(MOSI) <- ESP MOSI17`, `D9(MISO) -> ESP MISO18`, `D7(CS) <- ESP CS19`, GND common.
  - Verify on the board that Daisy `D13` (IRQ to ESP) is physically wired to ESP GPIO `8` (your `WAVEX_ESP_DAISY_IRQ`). Your logs disagree: Daisy prints IRQ=ASSERTED (active-low) while ESP reads level=1. That suggests wrong pin, missing pull-up, or inverted sense on ESP.
- Ensure no pin conflicts
  - I disabled the SD SPI block in `main.cpp` so it won’t reconfigure `D9`. Power-cycle Daisy after flashing to guarantee a clean AF state.
- Force MISO back to SPI AF after init
  - Add a one-time MISO re-init after `Spi_Init(hw, &spi_handle)` returns, to guarantee PB4/`D9` is AF5 SPI1 and not left as GPIO by any earlier code:
```cpp
// After WaveX::Comm::Spi_Init(hw, &spi_handle);
__HAL_RCC_GPIOB_CLK_ENABLE();
GPIO_InitTypeDef GPIO_InitStruct = {0};
GPIO_InitStruct.Pin = GPIO_PIN_4;                 // PB4 = D9
GPIO_InitStruct.Mode = GPIO_MODE_AF_PP;
GPIO_InitStruct.Pull = GPIO_NOPULL;
GPIO_InitStruct.Speed = GPIO_SPEED_FREQ_VERY_HIGH;
GPIO_InitStruct.Alternate = GPIO_AF5_SPI1;        // SPI1 MISO
HAL_GPIO_Init(GPIOB, &GPIO_InitStruct);
hw.PrintLine("Re-forced D9/PB4 to AF5 (SPI1 MISO) after SPI init");
```
- Reset Daisy SPI state before ESP reads
  - Call `WaveX::Comm::Spi_ForceReset();` once after init and before sending SYNC to ensure DMA is armed fresh, IRQ released (if needed), and `g_packet_ready` coherent.

If still 0xFF after the above:
- Start DMA on CS falling edge
  - LibDaisy SPI slave often needs the DMA arming to happen when NSS goes low. Your current code arms DMA continuously; the master may clock before the peripheral has loaded valid TX. The robust route is the HAL SPI3 path you already have (`inter_spi_hal.*`) which:
    - Uses EXTI on NSS to arm `HAL_SPI_TransmitReceive_DMA` per transaction.
    - Double-buffers TX/RX.
  - To try it quickly (for bring-up), swap to HAL path in `main.cpp`:
    - Include `comm/inter_spi_hal.h`
    - Call `WaveX::Comm::SpiHal_Init(hw);`
    - In your main loop, call `WaveX::Comm::SpiHal_Service();`
    - Temporarily skip the `WaveX::Comm::Spi_*` API calls while validating the electrical/transfer side. Once you see non-FF bytes on ESP32, we can bridge the HAL layer into your `Spi_*` API.

Extra checks:
- ESP32 SPI settings: Mode 0, MSB first, CS idle high with clean falling edge. Keep small clock (e.g., 1–2 MHz) for bring-up.
- Use a logic analyzer if available: during a transfer with CS low, confirm D9 toggles. If it stays high, MISO isn’t under SPI control.

Status: I removed the SD SPI init that conflicted with `D9`. Next, I recommend forcing D9 back to AF5 after `Spi_Init`, power-cycling, verifying IRQ wiring/polarity, then re-testing. If still failing, switch to the HAL SPI3 path that arms DMA on CS falling edge.

- Changes made: Disabled SD SPI init block in `firmware/daisy/src/main.cpp` to prevent MISO conflicts.

Model: GPT-5 Code Assistant, 32k, Multimodal (Code), rev 2025-08-15

- SPI rate: 10 MHz (set on the ESP32 master). Daisy is SPI slave; its prescaler is ignored in slave mode.
- Yes, we’re currently using libDaisy SPI on SPI1 (pins D8 SCK, D10 MOSI, D9 MISO, D7 CS), not the raw HAL SPI3 path.

To downclock (recommended on a breadboard):
- Change the ESP32 clock setting in `firmware/shared/config/pin_config.h`:
  - `WAVEX_ESP_SPI_CLK_HZ` from `10000000` to e.g. `1000000` (1 MHz), then rebuild/flash the ESP32.
- No Daisy change is needed for the rate; it follows the master.

Model: GPT-5 Code Assistant, 32k, Multimodal (Code), rev 2025-08-15

Quick update: I checked the SPI protocol header (`pkt_t`) and pin config; Daisy is on libDaisy SPI1. Your new logs show zeros because the slave DMA was armed with a zeroed TX buffer before the packet was ready.

- SPI rate: Master (ESP32) is set to 10 MHz. Daisy is a slave; its prescaler is ignored.
- Downclock: Change `WAVEX_ESP_SPI_CLK_HZ` in `firmware/shared/config/pin_config.h` from 10000000 to 1000000 (1 MHz), rebuild/flash ESP32.

Why you’re seeing 00 00…:
- We start SPI DMA on Daisy immediately at init with a zeroed `g_tx_buffer`.
- When ESP32 does an initial 8-byte “poke,” it clocks out those zeros before we’ve restarted DMA with your SYNC packet.

Fast fix on Daisy (keep SPI1/libDaisy):
- After queueing a packet and asserting IRQ, immediately re-prime/restart DMA so the first bytes shifted match the new TX buffer.

Suggested edit in `firmware/daisy/src/comm/inter_spi.cpp`, inside `Spi_Send` right after `irq_assert()`:
```cpp
// Ensure DMA uses the freshly prepared TX buffer
g_dma_setup = false;
prime_tx_and_start_dma();
```

Then:
- Power-cycle Daisy after flashing (to clear any stale pin states).
- Re-test at 1 MHz. ESP’s small 8-byte read after IRQ should now show a non-zero header (e.g., len=1).

Note:
- We’re currently using libDaisy SPI1 (D8 SCK, D10 MOSI, D9 MISO, D7 CS) as wired to the ESP32. If bring-up remains flaky, the robust alternative is the raw HAL path with NSS-falling-edge arming, but SPI1 with libDaisy should work once we restart DMA when data becomes ready.

I checked `Spi_Send` and confirmed DMA is now started before asserting IRQ. Your ESP32 reads still show 00s, which likely means the master begins clocking before SPI1’s TX FIFO is primed.

- What you’re seeing: Daisy reports PacketReady=1, DmaSetup=1, IRQ=ASSERTED; ESP32 reads 00 00… and len=0. That’s consistent with the master clocking immediately and sampling zeros from the TX FIFO before DMA fills it.

- Optional debug: On Daisy, log the first 8 bytes of `g_tx_buffer` just before starting DMA so we can compare with what ESP32 reads.

Answers:

We zero-pad the remainder so the master won’t see stale data.
- Padding heartbeat: Max payload is 240 bytes. You can pad heartbeat payloads up to 240 bytes if you want stronger on-wire visibility during bring-up, but it’s not required. The header’s `len` will reflect the true length; the rest will be zero. The master should always read the full 246-byte frame and parse according to `len`.

- Your Daisy header shows 01 00 00 00 …, so the TX buffer is correct. ESP32 reading 00s means the master is clocking before the slave’s TX FIFO is actually being shifted onto MISO.
- We’re using libDaisy SPI1 (D8/D10/D9/D7). Slave bit rate is set by the master; we downclock on ESP32 (set WAVEX_ESP_SPI_CLK_HZ=1 MHz).

Do this on ESP32 (simple, reliable fix):
- When Daisy IRQ is low, do a single full transfer of 246 bytes (sizeof(pkt_t)). Don’t do the 8-byte “probe” first. The short probe can hit a timing window where the TX FIFO hasn’t presented the header yet, so you read zeros and then classify it as empty.
- Keep your periodic “poke” only for when IRQ is high (idle), not when it’s low.

Answering your padding question:
- pkt_t is a fixed 246-byte frame: 2-byte len, 2-byte type, 240-byte payload, 2-byte crc.
- You cannot send “>246 bytes”. Max payload is 240. We already zero-pad unused payload so you don’t need to “accumulate” multiple heartbeats; one frame carries whatever len you set (e.g., 12). The master must always read all 246 bytes and then use h.len to know how many payload bytes are meaningful.

If it still reads 00s after the change:
- Verify wiring (SPI1: D8=SCK, D10=MOSI, D9=MISO, D7=CS), and scope/LA MISO during CS low; you should see nonzero toggling.
- Keep the ESP32 at 1 MHz for bring-up.
- We can add a short 1–2 ms guard between DMA start and IRQ assert (already added) and tune it down later.
- If you want bulletproof timing: switch Daisy to raw HAL SPI1 with EXTI on NSS and Start DMA on CS falling edge. That’s the simplest slave design electrically and timing-wise. I can implement that next if you want.

```diff
-    stats.dma_rx_complete++;
-    
-    // Process the received data in the opposite buffer
-    uint8_t *done_rx = useA ? rxB : rxA;
-    process_rx_frame(done_rx);
-}
+void SpiHal_TxRxCpltCallback(SPI_HandleTypeDef *hspi)
+{
+    if (hspi != &hspi1) return;
+    
+    transfer_active = false;
+    stats.dma_tx_complete++;
+    stats.dma_rx_complete++;
+    
+    // After TX completes, if we had queued data, clear flag and release IRQ
+    if (tx_ready) {
+        tx_ready = false;
+        irq_release_hi();
+    }
+    
+    // Process the received data in the opposite buffer
+    uint8_t *done_rx = useA ? rxB : rxA;
+    process_rx_frame(done_rx);
+}
*** End Patch
PATCH
```
